input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A commercial building whose physical assets are represented as a cyber‑physical system. The Digital Twin’s core model is a BIM (Building Information Model) enriched with device‑level definitions for sensors, actuators, and controllers (BCS components). This representation captures the building’s environmental, mechanical, and operational assets and enables real‑time monitoring of their state changes via continuous data exchange with the physical infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The framework provides real‑time monitoring of the building’s physical state, remote control of embedded devices and controllers defined within BIM models, immersive visualization interfaces for operators, and analytics services that can be extended by custom plugins for predictive optimisation and bespoke analytics.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The digital twin is built on a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline that extracts structured representations from BIM‑enriched models; a real‑time data‑exchange layer keeps the physical building and the digital representation synchronized; a modular architecture with standardized extension points allows developers to add custom services, analytics, and control logic; immersive visualization interfaces enable monitoring and control of the twin; the framework supports integration of domain‑specific plugins and analytics to evolve alongside operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Continuous real‑time bidirectional synchronization between physical building sensors and the digital twin, providing immediate data consistency as sensor streams become available. No explicit latency or update‑interval thresholds are defined in the documentation; the system is engineered to operate in a real‑time, continuous mode through its real‑time data exchange capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture supports a scalable constellation of twin instances, ranging from individual component builders to entire infrastructure systems, all managed within a centralized DT‑Framework hub that coordinates data flow and responsibility delegation; although the documents do not describe decentralized deployments, they emphasize the ability to host many twin instances under a single orchestrated environment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The orchestration follows a modular hub‑and‑spoke pattern, where the DT‑Framework acts as the central coordinator, exposing standardized extension points for custom services, analytics, and integration modules; runtime configuration management allows on‑the‑fly adjustment of services without full redeployment; the system realizes bidirectional data exchange with the physical building and directly supports the integration of BIM, AI, and sensor network components.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The framework emphasizes interoperability across heterogeneous systems, providing standardized extension points that allow integration with external services such as BIM tools, sensor networks, AI analytics, and potentially other enterprise systems; the system’s design is agnostic to specific data exchange protocols, but it supports plug‑in modules that adhere to common standards, facilitating horizontal integration with other Digital Twins or organizational workflows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system uses a model‑driven workflow that enriches BIM models with device metadata (for example, photometric values for lighting). Building control engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a unified semantic foundation. The enriched BIM models are transformed through Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) techniques into structured representations that drive the deployment of Digital‑Twin artifacts. Runtime data are exchanged via the Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto middleware: sensor values are streamed to Ditto, which persists events and exposes them through standardized APIs, while actuator commands are issued through Ditto and routed to the physical devices.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The twin targets lighting and HVAC assets. The LightTwinComponent in Unreal Engine creates a visual representation and processes photometric values from the BIM model. Actuation is performed through Ditto‑backed MQTT messaging: a message containing 'on', 'brightness', or 'colortemp' is sent to the twin, routed to the physical lighting device via the DittoController, and executed by the device. Remote control is mediated by a UE5 Delegates/Observable pattern that triggers command propagation. Safety constraints are enforced using Ditto policy validation and pre‑execution checks that verify command ranges and unit consistency before delivery to the device.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Data integrity is ensured through Eclipse Ditto’s policy‑based validation, which checks sensor values against defined ranges and units, and through pre‑execution checks for actuator commands to avoid unsafe operations. Fault tolerance is achieved via Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery guarantee and Ditto’s persistent event storage, guaranteeing that messages are neither lost nor processed out of order, thereby maintaining synchronization and operational safety between the physical assets and their digital counterparts.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system captures sensor data from physical assets and streams it into the digital twin using a real‑time, bidirectional data exchange pipeline. Input from the building is ingested through the framework’s integration of Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, which act as middleware for continuous synchronization. Data are routed along the established connection between the physical environment and the digital twin, enabling live monitoring of status, performance, and events within the BIM context.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Commands originating from the digital twin are routed back to the physical building through the same Eclipse Hono/Ditto channel used for telemetry. Facility Managers, acting as operators, issue control messages via the DT’s interactive interface; these messages target specific equipment endpoints identified by BIM element IDs and use JSON payloads that specify command types (e.g., actuator override, HVAC setpoint adjustment, lighting level change). The underlying framework forwards these control messages to the physical devices, and confirmation of command execution is reported back to the DT through the broker, allowing the digital model to reflect the updated physical state. The system supports bidirectional communication but does not specify conflict‑resolution logic or explicit authentication mechanisms beyond those implied by the use of the secure Hono broker.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The network backbone of the digital twin framework relies on Eclipse Hono as a lightweight, MQTT‑based messaging broker and Eclipse Ditto for twin data representation and change dissemination. Hono operates over TLS‑secured TCP connections, providing real‑time publish/subscribe capabilities that ensure data from physical devices reach their respective Ditto twins in near‑zero‑latency. The architecture is inherently topic‑driven, with each BIM element corresponding to a unique Hono topic; Ditto maintains a registry of these topics to enable efficient twin updates and queries. Network requirements are modest, as the messaging protocol is designed for IoT‑scale traffic, but the framework assumes a minimum bandwidth to support continuous sensor streams and the bidirectional flow of control messages. Reliability is achieved through Hono’s internal persistence layer and message replay mechanisms, while security is enforced by TLS encryption and mutual authentication at the broker level.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The model‑driven DT deployment framework implements a clear separation of the twin lifecycle into three core stages: 1) **Design & Enrichment** – Building Control Engineers model devices, sensors, actuators and controllers directly inside BIM, extending the BIM model with semantics for control logic; 2) **Model‑Driven Transformation** – A formal MDE pipeline automatically extracts the enriched BIM into structured runtime artefacts (service definitions, data schemas, connectivity descriptors); 3) **Operation & Evolution** – The generated artefacts are deployed onto a DT platform where real‑time data exchange synchronizes the physical building with its digital counterpart, and DT Engineers can plug in custom services through well‑defined extension points. Transition management is handled by the automated MDE pipeline, ensuring that each enriched BIM change triggers a reproducible, versioned deployment cycle with minimal manual intervention, thereby embedding lifecycle governance in the transformation workflow.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Twinning proceeds through a role‑centric, collaborative workflow: Building Control Engineers initiate the process by modelling devices and defining control logic in BIM; the MDE pipeline creates deployable DT artefacts; Facility Managers interact with the twin during runtime to monitor and steer operations; DT Engineers extend the twin by developing and integrating pluggable services or analytics modules via standardized extension points. The modular architecture permits iterative refinement—new modules are versioned and deployed without disrupting existing components, enabling continuous evolution of the twin’s functionality.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real‑time data exchange feeds synchronized sensor and actuator streams into the DT platform, where an immersive visualization interface lets Facility Managers observe live operational states and adjust control logic on demand. This immediate visibility empowers operational decision‑making by revealing deviations from planned behavior and enabling on‑the‑fly adjustments to control strategies.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework adopts rigorous standardization by formalizing BIM semantics within its MDE pipeline and providing standardized extension points that align with open‑source DT platforms such as Eclipse Ditto and BaSyx. These extensions adhere to interoperability norms required by AECO domains, thereby facilitating seamless integration between the twin’s semantic models and external services.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the system as a building (specifically a Living Lab in the text, generalized here) represented as a cyber-physical system using BIM enriched with BCS components. It correctly identifies the inclusion of sensors, actuators, and controllers within the model and the goal of real-time monitoring. Supported by quotes: "DTs are cyber-physical information constructs that... creates a virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building)", "embedding domain-specific control semantics... into BIM models", and "define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment".

The extraction accurately describes the services provided, including real-time monitoring, remote control, immersive visualization, and extensible analytics. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'mechanisms to feed control signals back to the BCS', and 'interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control'.

The description correctly identifies the core technologies and architectural features, such as the MDE pipeline, BIM integration, real-time synchronization tools, and visualization engine. Supported by quotes: 'model-driven framework... grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', and 'Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system's focus on real-time synchronization and correctly notes the absence of specific latency metrics in the text. Supported by quotes: 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins', and 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The extraction accurately describes the centralized architecture and scalability. It is supported by the text's mention of 'scalable DT deployment' and 'scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems.' The concept of the 'DT‑Framework hub' aligns with the description of the framework acting as a 'central hub.' The extraction correctly notes that decentralized deployments are not described, which is consistent with the source's focus on centralized coordination.

This extraction is highly detailed and well-grounded. The 'hub‑and‑spoke pattern' corresponds to the framework being a 'central hub' and coordinator. 'Standardized extension points' are explicitly mentioned as a feature. 'Runtime configuration management' that allows 'adjustments without requiring full redeployment' is directly supported. The mention of 'bidirectional data exchange' and integration of 'BIM, AI, and sensor network components' is explicitly stated in the sources.

The extraction correctly identifies the emphasis on interoperability and the use of 'standardized extension points' to integrate with external services like 'BIM tools, sensor networks, AI analytics.' This is supported by the text. However, the claim that the design is 'agnostic to specific data exchange protocols' is speculative; the text mentions specific technologies like 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto' for connectivity without claiming broad agnosticism. The rest of the description regarding plug-in modules and standards is well-supported.

Our framework ensures data integrity through Eclipse

and units for sensor data, and pre-execution checks for actuator commands to prevent conflicts or unsafe operations.
Fault tolerance is provided by Eclipse Hono’s at-least-once
message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience against network failures or service disruptions.
These mechanisms collectively maintain synchronization and
operational safety between physical assets and their DTs.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the model-driven workflow described, highlighting BIM enrichment and MDE transformation, supported by the text stating 'model-driven framework... grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)' and 'framework then leverages MDE principles'. It correctly identifies Eclipse Hono and Ditto for runtime data exchange. However, it includes specific details not present in the source snippets, such as 'photometric values' (text only mentions 'lighting systems') and the specific behavior of Ditto persisting events.

The description provides highly specific technical details—such as 'LightTwinComponent', specific message fields like 'on', 'brightness', 'colortemp', 'DittoController', and 'UE5 Delegates/Observable pattern'—which are completely absent from the provided text. The source mentions 'lighting systems' and high-level middleware usage, but does not contain the specific component names or implementation logic described.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly discusses sensors. It mentions 'sensor configurations' being embedded into BIM models and enabling engineers to 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment'. Since evidence exists, the 'Not in Document' claim is incorrect.

The extraction attributes specific fidelity mechanisms to the middleware, such as 'Eclipse Ditto’s policy-based validation' and 'Eclipse Hono’s at-least-once message delivery guarantee'. While the text mentions 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization' using these tools, it does not provide the technical details regarding message delivery guarantees or validation policies found in the description.

IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific

— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
